comment_text.sn21.1:1.3: Kolita was his personal name, Moggallāna his clan name.
comment_text.sn21.1:2.7: So-called because of the absence of vitakka and vicāra, which are the precursors of speech (SN 41.6:2.4, MN 44:15.3).
comment_text.sn21.1:2.8: The verb viharati here and below, and in the parallel passages at SN 40.1ff., uses the aorist and present tenses inconsistently. I take the first passage as historical present, the second as aorist in simple past tense.
comment_text.sn21.1:2.9: In the Suttas, saññāmanasikārā is only used in negative contexts, but later texts use it in a positive way as well (Ps 1.1:171.5, Pe 8:3.3).
comment_text.sn21.1:3.5: A series of similar conversations on different states of meditation is found in the Moggallānasaṁyutta (SN 40).

root_text.sn21.1:1.2: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā sāvatthiyaṁ viharati jetavane anāthapiṇḍikassa ārāme.
root_text.sn21.1:1.3: Tatra kho āyasmā mahāmoggallāno bhikkhū āmantesi:
root_text.sn21.1:1.5: “Āvuso”ti kho te bhikkhū āyasmato mahāmoggallānassa paccassosuṁ.
root_text.sn21.1:2.2: “idha mayhaṁ, āvuso, rahogatassa paṭisallīnassa evaṁ cetaso parivitakko udapādi:
root_text.sn21.1:2.6: ‘idha bhikkhu vitakkavicārānaṁ vūpasamā ajjhattaṁ sampasādanaṁ cetaso ekodibhāvaṁ avitakkaṁ avicāraṁ samādhijaṁ pītisukhaṁ dutiyaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja viharati.
root_text.sn21.1:2.8: So khvāhaṁ, āvuso, vitakkavicārānaṁ vūpasamā ajjhattaṁ sampasādanaṁ cetaso ekodibhāvaṁ avitakkaṁ avicāraṁ samādhijaṁ pītisukhaṁ dutiyaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja vihariṁ.
root_text.sn21.1:2.9: Tassa mayhaṁ, āvuso, iminā vihārena viharato vitakkasahagatā saññāmanasikārā samudācaranti.
root_text.sn21.1:3.1: Atha kho maṁ, āvuso, bhagavā iddhiyā upasaṅkamitvā etadavoca:
root_text.sn21.1:3.2: ‘moggallāna moggallāna, mā, brāhmaṇa, ariyaṁ tuṇhībhāvaṁ pamādo, ariye tuṇhībhāve cittaṁ saṇṭhapehi, ariye tuṇhībhāve cittaṁ ekodibhāvaṁ karohi, ariye tuṇhībhāve cittaṁ samādahā’ti.
root_text.sn21.1:3.3: So khvāhaṁ, āvuso, aparena samayena vitakkavicārānaṁ vūpasamā ajjhattaṁ sampasādanaṁ cetaso ekodibhāvaṁ avitakkaṁ avicāraṁ samādhijaṁ pītisukhaṁ dutiyaṁ jhānaṁ upasampajja viharāmi.
root_text.sn21.1:3.4: Yañhi taṁ, āvuso, sammā vadamāno vadeyya:
root_text.sn21.1:3.5: ‘satthārā anuggahito sāvako mahābhiññataṁ patto’ti, mamaṁ taṁ sammā vadamāno vadeyya:

translation_text.sn21.1:1.2: At one time the Buddha was staying near Sāvatthī in Jeta’s Grove, Anāthapiṇḍika’s monastery.
translation_text.sn21.1:1.3: There Venerable Mahāmoggallāna addressed the mendicants:
translation_text.sn21.1:2.2: “Just now, reverends, as I was in private retreat this thought came to mind:
translation_text.sn21.1:2.3: ‘They speak of this thing called “noble silence”.
translation_text.sn21.1:2.4: What then is this noble silence?’
translation_text.sn21.1:2.6: ‘As the placing of the mind and keeping it connected are stilled, a mendicant enters and remains in the second absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of immersion, with internal clarity and mind at one, without placing the mind and keeping it connected.
translation_text.sn21.1:2.8: And so, as the placing of the mind and keeping it connected were stilled, I was entering and remaining in the second absorption, which has the rapture and bliss born of immersion, with internal clarity and mind at one, without placing the mind and keeping it connected.
translation_text.sn21.1:2.9: While I was in that meditation, perception and focus accompanied by placing the mind beset me.
translation_text.sn21.1:3.1: Then the Buddha came up to me with his psychic power and said,
translation_text.sn21.1:3.2: ‘Moggallāna, Moggallāna! Don’t neglect noble silence, brahmin! Settle your mind in noble silence; unify your mind and bring it to immersion in noble silence.’
translation_text.sn21.1:3.3: And so, after some time, as the placing of the mind and keeping it connected were stilled, I entered and remained in the second absorption …
translation_text.sn21.1:3.4: So if anyone should be rightly called
translation_text.sn21.1:3.5: a disciple who attained to great direct knowledge with help from the Teacher, it’s me.”
